_metadata.purpose: Maps common Hausa security/fraud-related terms to their canonical English keyword equivalents, for use in multilingual scam/threat detection.
_metadata.disclaimer: This is a starter, community-style vocabulary, not an exhaustive or professionally certified translation. Confidence levels are noted per entry. Corrections and native-speaker review are welcome, especially for entries marked 'community' or 'uncertain'.

confidence_levels.verified: Cross-checked against a published Hausa-English dictionary or multiple independent sources.
confidence_levels.common: Widely known/standard vocabulary, high confidence, not individually cross-checked in a dictionary this session.
confidence_levels.uncertain: Included from the original source list but could not be confirmed; flagged for native-speaker review.

notes: Hausa has three hooked/glottalized letters not on standard keyboards: ɓ, ɗ, ƙ. Where relevant, both the correct spelling and the common ASCII fallback (b, d, k) are included as separate keys mapping to the same English value, since real-world user input usually omits the hooks.

'tafashe' from the original list is corrected here: it appears to derive from 'tafasa' (to boil), not 'broken'. It is kept below marked as uncertain/deprecated, with 'lalace' and 'karye' provided as the verified replacements for 'broken/spoiled'.

stopwords.an: Passive/impersonal verb marker (e.g. 'an yi masa zamba' = 'he was defrauded'). Strip before matching.
stopwords.yi: 'Do/did' — light verb often paired with a noun (yi zamba = commit fraud, yi hack = to hack). Strip before matching unless part of a fixed phrase.
stopwords.na: Possessive/linking particle ('of', masculine) or 'I' depending on context.
stopwords.ta: Possessive/linking particle ('of', feminine) or possessive suffix ('my', feminine noun, e.g. wayata = my phone).

an hack.note: 'an' + loanword 'hack', very common construction in spoken Hausa.

sim swap.note: Usually kept as an English loan phrase.

malware.note: Common loanword, no established native equivalent.

scam.note: Loanword; 'zamba' is the native equivalent, see below.

fraud.note: Loanword; 'zamba' is the native equivalent, see below.

ɗan damfara.note: Correct hooked-letter spelling of the entry above.

sata.note: Dictionary-confirmed: unlawful taking of property. 'an sata' (was stolen) reduces to this once the 'an' stopword is stripped.

lambar sirri.note: Literally 'secret number' — used for PIN codes.

hari.note: e.g. 'hari ta yanar gizo' = cyber attack.

yanar gizo.note: Literally 'spider web' — the common phrase for internet/web.

link.note: Usually kept as an English loanword in spoken Hausa.

buga.note: 'To type/enter' e.g. 'buga lambar sirri' = enter your PIN.

efcc.note: Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (Nigeria) — kept as-is, commonly referenced in fraud reports.

tafashe.note: DEPRECATED / UNVERIFIED — kept from the original list for backward compatibility, but 'tafasa' appears to mean 'to boil' in published Hausa dictionaries, not 'broken'. Prefer 'lalace' or 'karye' below. Needs native-speaker confirmation.

karye.note: To snap/break (physical breakage, e.g. a cracked screen).
